Our puzzle-desk has a quick rule of thumb: the two most-connected hub circles are the easiest to keep separated from everything if you nail them to the two extreme digits, so pin the busiest circle to 1 and the next-busiest to 8 and let the rest fall out. If we lock the layout to that hubs-take-the-extremes rule, does the digit total move off what the full optimizer finds?
manual.constraint:
from collections import defaultdict
model = load_model('marilyn_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'hubs take the extremes': rank circles by adjacency degree and pin the two
# most-connected hub circles to the extreme digits 1 and 8 (a high-degree circle is
# easiest to keep >=gap from all its neighbors at an extreme value); the solver fills
# the remaining circles around those two anchors.
deg = defaultdict(int)
for (c, cc) in model.net:
    deg[c] += 1
hubs = sorted(model.c, key=lambda c: (-deg[c], c))[:2]
model.x[hubs[0]].fix(1)
model.x[hubs[1]].fix(8)
description = 'Heuristic: pin the two hub circles to the extreme digits 1 and 8'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'marilyn_mip__heur_hub', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['marilyn_mip__heur_hub']['obj']['value']))

Instead of a full optimization, our operators want a dead-simple rule: walk the nodes in order and drop each one onto whichever side gives the bigger immediate gain against the neighbors already placed. If we lock the partition to that one-pass greedy assignment, how much cut weight do we leave on the table versus the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('maxcut_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'greedy one-pass': visit nodes in index order; place each on whichever side
# maximizes the net weight of cut edges to its already-placed neighbors, then fix x.
import collections
adj = collections.defaultdict(list)
for (i, j) in model.e:
    wij = value(model.w[i, j])
    adj[i].append((j, wij)); adj[j].append((i, wij))
side = {}
for nd in model.n:
    gain_if_1 = sum(w for (nb, w) in adj[nd] if nb in side and side[nb] == 0)
    gain_if_0 = sum(w for (nb, w) in adj[nd] if nb in side and side[nb] == 1)
    side[nd] = 1 if gain_if_1 >= gain_if_0 else 0
for nd in model.n:
    model.x[nd].fix(side[nd])
description = 'Heuristic: greedy one-pass node placement'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'maxcut_mip__heur_greedy', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['maxcut_mip__heur_greedy']['obj']['value']))

manual.constraint:
model = load_model('mexls_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic export-quota policy: cap each mill's total exports at a share of the
# overall export ceiling etot, proportional to its steel-furnace (open-hearth +
# BOF + electric-arc) capacity.
steel_units = ['openhearth', 'bof', 'elec-arc']
scap = {s: sum(value(model.ks[u, s]) for u in steel_units if (u, s) in model.ks) for s in model.iss}
tot = sum(scap.values())
etot_val = value(model.etot)
model.export_quota = ConstraintList()
for s in model.iss:
    model.export_quota.add(sum(model.e[c, s] for c in model.ce) <= etot_val * scap[s] / tot)
description = 'Heuristic: per-mill export quota proportional to steel-furnace capacity'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'mexls_lp__heur_export_quota', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Surface mining is going in patchy — some level-1 blocks are fully out while others sit at zero, which leaves an uneven pit floor for the crews working below. Shouldn't every level-1 block be extracted at least 40% to keep the floor reasonably flat? What's keeping the model from smoothing that surface pass out?

def _surface_floor_rule(mm, i, j):

    return mm.x[1, i, j] >= 0.40

manual.query: The plan hammers the front corner of every bench and barely touches the back corner, which is wearing the haul ramps unevenly. Couldn't we make the back-corner block carry at least a quarter of whatever the front-corner block gets on each level? What's stopping the model from balancing the two corners like that?
manual.constraint:

def _corner_parity_rule(mm, l):
    if (l, 1, 1) not in mm.D or (l, 4, 4) not in mm.D:
        return Constraint.Skip
    return mm.x[l, 4, 4] >= 0.25 * mm.x[l, 1, 1]

description = 'Why-not: back-corner block at least a quarter of the front-corner block per level'
new_version = 'mine_lp__corner_parity'

manual.query: Mobilizing and demobilizing the fleet twice is what eats us. Couldn't we just run a clean two-bench campaign — take the whole top bench and the whole second bench out at 100%, walk away, and skip the deep stuff entirely? Wouldn't committing to that two-pass plan up front beat all this fractional dithering on profit?
manual.constraint:

# Heuristic planning strategy: commit in advance to a two-bench campaign — fully
# extract every accessible block on levels 1 and 2 (x=1), and leave levels 3 and 4
# untouched (x=0). Then solve the residual under that fixed depth profile.
for (l, i, j) in model.D:
    if l in (1, 2):
        model.x[l, i, j].fix(1.0)
    else:
        model.x[l, i, j].fix(0.0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: two-bench campaign — extract levels 1 and 2 fully, skip levels 3 and 4'
new_version = 'mine_lp__heur_two_bench'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Our schedulers swear by a build-ahead rule for the finished good: get every AJ8172 run done in the front of the calendar and keep the tail end of the horizon clear as a service buffer. If we lock AJ8172 so none of its production lands in the last three buckets, how much worse does the weighted production total get versus the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:

# Heuristic 'build AJ8172 ahead': schedulers want the finished good produced in the front
# of the horizon, leaving the final stretch clear. Ban any AJ8172 production setup later
# than (card - 3) periods from the start, computed from the horizon length.
ord_ = {t: i + 1 for i, t in enumerate(model.TT)}
card = len(list(model.TT))
cutoff = card - 3
for t in model.TT:
    if ord_[t] > cutoff:
        model.d['AJ8172', t].fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: build AJ8172 ahead, no production setups in the final three periods'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'mrp2_mip__heur_build_ahead', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

Do we even need the layout solver? Just cram every activity into one big facility — fac-1 — and call it done. How much interaction cost does that lazy plan rack up?

manual.query: What about a dead-simple pairing rule — act-1 and act-2 in fac-1, act-3 and act-4 in fac-2, act-5 on its own in fac-3? How does that tidy little layout score on interaction cost?
Why churn through a full network optimization? Couldn't we just pre-commit each market to take half its volume off its single cheapest inbound lane and let the rest sort itself out?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('netgen_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'half-off-cheapest': pre-commit each market (positive-demand node) to take
# half of its volume off its single cheapest inbound lane (capacity permitting); the
# optimizer balances the remainder.
for n in model.node:
    dem = value(model.demand[n])
    if dem <= 0:
        continue
    in_arcs = [(i, j) for (i, j) in model.arc if j == n]
    if not in_arcs:
        continue
    cheapest = min(in_arcs, key=lambda a: value(model.cost[a]))
    load = min(0.5 * dem, value(model.capacity[cheapest]))
    model.x[cheapest].fix(load)
description = 'Heuristic: serve half of each market off its cheapest inbound lane'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'netgen_lp__heur_halfcheap', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['netgen_lp__heur_halfcheap']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: A planner's rule of thumb here is to diversify away from the first candidate and just commit to the last column in the superstructure. If we lock the build to that rule, how much more expensive is the master than the optimizer's pick?
manual.constraint:

# Heuristic 'build-the-last-candidate': commit to the highest-indexed candidate column
# only, switching every other column off.
cols = list(model.col)
for c in model.col:
    model.y[c].fix(1 if c == cols[-1] else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: build only the last candidate column'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'nonsharp_mip__heur_last', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['nonsharp_mip__heur_last']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our mine planners like a blunt rule: dig every pit to the same uniform depth, picking the shallowest common cut-off that still leaves enough rock to cover total demand, and leave everything below that line in the ground. If we lock the plan to that single uniform-depth pattern, how much worse is the discounted net income than the optimizer's tailored schedule?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('openpit_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'uniform extraction depth': cap every pit at the same maximum depth K.
# K = the shallowest common cut-off whose combined volume over all pits still clears
# the horizon's total demand; force every segment below K shut in all pits, then let
# the solver set the extraction volumes on the fixed open pattern.
slist = list(model.s)
total_demand = sum(value(model.demand[t]) for t in model.t)
K = len(slist)
for k in range(1, len(slist) + 1):
    cap = sum(sum(value(model.evo[p, slist[i]]) for i in range(k)) for p in model.p)
    if cap >= total_demand:
        K = k
        break
for p in model.p:
    for i in range(len(slist)):
        if i + 1 > K:
            model.open[p, slist[i]].fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: uniform extraction-depth cap at the shallowest demand-feasible depth'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'openpit_mip__heur_unifdepth', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['openpit_mip__heur_unifdepth']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: The cabinet wants a smoother investment build-up — total investment in any year should be at least 5% above the previous year's, beyond the existing non-decreasing rule, so the build-out doesn't stagnate mid-plan. How does that minimum-growth floor on investment shift the development plan and the welfare outcome?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('pak_lp', models_dictionary)
te = list(model.te)
def inv_growth_rule(model, i):
    if i < len(te) - 1:
        return model.ti[te[i+1]] >= 1.05 * model.ti[te[i]]
    return Constraint.Skip
model.inv_growth_floor = Constraint(range(len(te)), rule=inv_growth_rule)
description = 'What-if: total investment grows at least 5% year over year'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'pak_lp__inv_growth_5pct', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

Wouldn't a steadier, more front-loaded consumption path still land close to today's welfare?

te = list(model.te)
def cons_growth_rule(model, i):
    if i < len(te) - 1:

    return Constraint.Skip
model.cons_growth_floor = Constraint(range(len(te)), rule=cons_growth_rule)

manual.query: The plan leans hard on foreign aid early and then lets it taper off. Couldn't we require that net capital inflow `f[t]` in any single year stay within 30% above the horizon average, as a smoothing rule? Wouldn't a more even aid trajectory still come in close to today's welfare?

N = len(list(model.t))

    return model.f[t] <= 1.3 * (sum(model.f[tt] for tt in model.t) / N)

manual.query: Kraft is the highest-priced grade we make, so couldn't we just run a margin-tiered contract plan — pin the whole paper book to its tiers in advance: kraft pushed to its contractual ceiling, newsprint and printing held at their contractual floors — and let the solver settle the upstream pulp and wood flows from there? That leans as hard on the best-margin grade as the contracts allow. What's the tradeoff the model is seeing that I'm not?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('paperco_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic planning strategy: a margin-tiered contract plan fixed in advance, then solve the residual.
# Assign every paper grade to a contract tier up front -- best-margin grade (kraft) to its ceiling,
# the rest to their contractual floors -- then let the solver settle the upstream pulp and wood flows.
tier_plan = {
    'kraft': model.paper_ub,      # best-margin grade -> contractual ceiling
    'newsprint': model.paper_lb,  # held at contractual floor
    'printing': model.paper_lb,   # held at contractual floor
}
for q, bound in tier_plan.items():
    model.paper[q].fix(value(bound[q]))
description = 'Why-not heuristic: margin-tiered contract plan -- kraft at ceiling, newsprint and printing at their contractual floors'
new_version = 'paperco_lp__heur_kraft_focused'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Do we really need to optimize a whole branching route tree? Couldn't each source just funnel its entire downstream flow through its single nearest gateway port and let the legs cascade from there? Run that simple one-gateway-per-source rule and tell me what mileage it costs us.
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('phosdis_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic single-gateway policy: each source must reach all (N-1) other nodes, so push its
# entire first-hop outflow (N-1 units) through its single cheapest-distance outgoing arc and
# send nothing on its other direct legs; let the solver cascade onward from the gateway.
N = len(list(model.nodes))
for s in model.src:
    outs = sorted([(b, value(model.darc[s, b])) for (a, b) in model.arcs if a == s], key=lambda z: z[1])
    gateway = outs[0][0]
    for (a, b) in model.arcs:
        if a == s:
            model.x[s, a, b].fix(float(N - 1) if b == gateway else 0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: route each source through its single cheapest gateway'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'phosdis_lp__heur_gateway', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['phosdis_lp__heur_gateway']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why grind through an optimizer for this? Couldn't we just play it safe and put the whole book into the two lowest-rated government bonds, us-ser-e and us-ser-f, splitting between them in proportion to their after-tax yield?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('port_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic risk-averse policy: hold only the two lowest-rated (safest) government bonds,
# us-ser-e and us-ser-f, splitting the full budget by their after-tax yield.
safe = ['us-ser-e', 'us-ser-f']
atax = {b: value(model.yld[b]) / 100 * (1 - value(model.taxrate[b])) for b in safe}
tot = sum(atax.values())
budget = 10.0
for b in model.b:
    model.investment[b].fix(budget * atax[b] / tot if b in safe else 0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: safe-government split by after-tax yield'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'port_lp__heur_safe_gov', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['port_lp__heur_safe_gov']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Running four grades through these workstations means constant setup churn. Couldn't we just rank the catalog by unit margin and commit the lines to the top two earners — class-4 at $40 and class-2 at $20 — and shelve the two thin grades, class-1 and class-3, then let the solver settle the exact volumes? Wouldn't that lean margin-first plan hold up against the spread-everything-thin mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('prodsp_lp', models_dictionary)
# Margin-priority heuristic: rank the catalog by unit margin (class-4=40, class-2=20,
# class-3=18, class-1=12), commit the lines to the top-two earners and shelve the two
# thinnest grades in advance, then solve the residual for the surviving grades.
shelved = ['class-1', 'class-3']  # two lowest-margin grades
for g in shelved:
    model.x[g].fix(0.0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: margin-rank the catalog, run only the top-two grades (class-4, class-2), shelve class-1 + class-3'
new_version = 'prodsp_lp__margin_top2_heuristic'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Why grind through the optimization at all? Couldn't we just weight each stock in proportion to its mean return — load up on the higher-return names and skip the negative ones — and call it a day?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('qp5_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: weight each stock in proportion to its (non-negative) mean return,
# normalized so the weights sum to 1. Negative-mean stocks get zero weight.
pos_mean = {s: max(value(model.mean[s]), 0.0) for s in model.s}
tot = sum(pos_mean.values())
for s in model.s:
    model.x[s].fix(pos_mean[s] / tot)
description = 'Heuristic: weights proportional to non-negative mean return'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'qp5_lp__heur_meanwt', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['qp5_lp__heur_meanwt']['obj']['value']))

Our depot dispatchers like a dead-simple flexibility rule: keep at least two of the small tu1 units stabled overnight at every depot, no matter what, so each yard has a couple of nimble units ready in the morning. If we lock the plan to that rule, how much more does the overnight stabling cost than the optimizer's leaner mix?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('railcirc_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'two-tu1-per-depot flex reserve': insist on at least two small tu1 units stabled
# overnight at every depot, computed as a fixed reserve of 2 per overnight (depot) arc, then
# let the solver size the rest of the fleet around that floor.
on_by_depot = {a[0]: a for a in model.on}
model.flex_reserve = ConstraintList()
for s in model.s:
    a = on_by_depot[s]
    model.flex_reserve.add(model.f['tu1', a[0], a[1], a[2], a[3]] >= 2)
description = 'Heuristic: two tu1 flex-reserve units stabled at every depot'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'railcirc_mip__heur_flex2', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['railcirc_mip__heur_flex2']['obj']['value']))

# Heuristic: a fixed ramp-up operating rule set in advance, then solve the residual.
# Ease the high grade up across the short horizon (10 -> 20 -> 30) instead of
# dumping it all into the final period; low/medium stay off; the period-4 residual
# slot carries nothing. Let the solver settle the stock balance from there.
ramp = {1: 10.0, 2: 20.0, 3: 30.0}
for t in model.t:
    model.x['high', t].fix(ramp[t])
    model.x['low', t].fix(0.0)
    model.x['medium', t].fix(0.0)
for p in model.p:
    model.x[p, 4].fix(0.0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: ramp-up plan on the high grade (10/20/30 across short periods); solve residual'
new_version = 'robert_lp__heuristic_ramp_high'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Why bother optimizing the dispatch hour by hour? Couldn't we just run both thermal units — coal and nuclear — flat out at their weekly capacity every hour and let the dam and the gap balance the rest?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('sddp_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic merit-blind policy: pin coal and nuclear to their full weekly capacity
# every hour (computed from wCapacity), leaving hydro/spill/reservoir/gap to balance.
HPW = 168
weeks = list(model.w)
def _week_of(t):
    return weeks[(int(t[1:]) - 1) // HPW]
for t in model.t:
    w = _week_of(t)
    model.X[t, 'HardCoal'].fix(value(model.wCapacity[w, 'HardCoal']))
    model.X[t, 'Nuclear'].fix(value(model.wCapacity[w, 'Nuclear']))
description = 'Heuristic: run coal and nuclear flat out at weekly capacity'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'sddp_lp__heur_thermal_flatout', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['sddp_lp__heur_thermal_flatout']['obj']['value']))

# Heuristic full-capacity policy: run every factory at capacity and split its output
# across the DCs in proportion to expected (probability-weighted) demand.
mean = {j: sum(value(model.scen_prob[s]) * value(model.scen_demand[s, j]) for s in model.S) for j in model.J}
total = sum(mean.values())
for i in model.I:
    for j in model.J:
        model.ship[i, j].fix(value(model.capacity[i]) * mean[j] / total)
description = 'Heuristic: full-capacity production, ship in proportion to expected demand'

totcap = sum(value(model.capacity[i]) for i in model.I)
for j in model.J:

Our layout team likes a no-fuss rule of thumb: give every row the shortest height band whose widest cell still fits within an even per-column share of the page, and only go taller if nothing fits that share. If we lock the table to that simple rule instead of the optimizer's mix, how much taller does the table end up?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('tablelayout_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'shortest-row-within-an-even-budget': allow each column an equal share of the
# page width (pw / number of columns), then give each row the SHORTEST height band whose
# widest cell still fits that even share; if none fits, fall back to the tallest (narrowest)
# band. Fix the height picks and let the solver set the resulting cell/column widths.
budget = value(model.pw) / len(model.C)
heights_of_row = {r: sorted(h for (rr, h) in model.rh if rr == r) for r in model.R}
for r in model.R:
    pick = None
    for h in heights_of_row[r]:
        widest = max(value(model.minRCHw[r, h, c]) for c in model.C)
        if widest <= budget:
            pick = h
            break
    if pick is None:
        pick = heights_of_row[r][-1]
    for h in heights_of_row[r]:
        model.bRH[r, h].fix(1 if h == pick else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: shortest row height that fits an even per-column width budget'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tablelayout_mip__heur', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['tablelayout_mip__heur']['obj']['value']))

fdmaize = value(model.fdmaize)
yc_maize = value(model.tmd['yield', 'maize'])
maize_area = fdmaize * nfam / yc_maize
for t in model.t:
    model.x[t, 'maize'].fix(maize_area)
    model.mat[t].fix(0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: cover maize demand with direct cropping only, no maize-after-tobacco'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tabora_lp__heur_maize_only', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(value(model.income)))

# Heuristic flat-build policy: replace optimized lumpy investment with an even capacity-build
# schedule. Size the annual build from the steady-state log throughput the forest can sustainably
# deliver: the existing-forest stock the mills draw on is capped by efs at
# sum_{s,k,u} iad[u,s]*scd[k]*land[s]/10 (1000 ha-equivalents); convert that at the average
# existing-forest yield yw to a total log volume, spread it evenly over the horizon, and split it
# between the two mills by their unit capacity-utilisation weight b.
nT = len(list(model.t))
yw_vals = [value(model.yw[te, u, s, cl]) for (te, u, s, cl) in model.yw if value(model.yw[te, u, s, cl]) > 0]
yw_mean = sum(yw_vals) / len(yw_vals)
cut_area = sum(value(model.iad[u, s]) * value(model.scd[k]) * value(model.land[s])
               for s in model.s for k in model.k for u in model.u) / 10.0
total_logs = cut_area * yw_mean
bw = {mm: sum(value(model.b[mm, p]) for p in model.p if (mm, p) in model.b) for mm in model.m}
tot_bw = sum(bw.values())
for mm in model.m:
    flat = (bw[mm] / tot_bw) * total_logs / nT
    for t in model.t:
        model.h[mm, t].fix(flat)
description = 'Heuristic: flat capacity build sized to steady-state log throughput, split by b'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tfordy_lp__heur_flatbuild', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['tfordy_lp__heur_flatbuild']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Forest authorities are pushing a biodiversity rule that brutia must stay the dominant species across the region — they want total brutia plantings to remain at least 1.5x the size of total nigra plantings going forward. What does the plan look like under that rule?

model.biodiversity = Constraint(

)

manual.query: Looking at the yield tables, pulplogs peak at age 20 across both species and across every site class — sawlogs don't really show up until much later rotations. Wouldn't managing every hectare on a clean 20-year rotation, routing the whole harvest through pulp and mothballing the saw-mill, give us a comparable total benefit?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('tforss_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic planning strategy set in advance, then solve the residual:
# put EVERY stand on one uniform 20-year rotation (pin all age classes other than
# a-20 to zero), mothball the saw-mill, and zero out sawnwood shipments. Let the
# solver settle the v[s,k,a-20] species/site split and the pulp processing levels
# within those pins.
for s in model.s:
    for k in model.k:
        for at in model.at:
            if at != 'a-20':
                model.v[s, k, at].fix(0.0)
model.h['saw-mill'].fix(0.0)
model.x['sawnwood'].fix(0.0)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: uniform 20-year rotation across every hectare, harvest routed through pulp, saw-mill mothballed'
new_version = 'tforss_lp__age20_pulp_only_heuristic'

manual.query: Our schedulers like a clean rule of thumb: take the single biggest port and haul all of it on its own dedicated direct voyage, no piggy-backing on shared multi-stop runs. If we lock Songkhla to its direct voyage v-04 that way, how much worse does the man-miles bill get versus the optimizer's mixed plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('thaix_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'direct-haul the biggest port': the largest-demand port (Songkhla) is carried
# only on its own dedicated direct voyage v-04; no shared multi-stop voyage lifts any of
# its men. The solver then sizes the remaining sailings around that fixed choice.
big_port = max(model.p, key=lambda pp: value(model.d[pp]))
direct = 'v-04'
for (vv, kk, qq) in model.vkp:
    if qq == big_port and vv != direct:
        model.y[vv, kk, qq].fix(0)
description = 'Heuristic: direct-haul the largest port on its dedicated voyage v-04'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'thaix_mip__heur_directbig', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['thaix_mip__heur_directbig']['obj']['value']))

for (x, tx, y, ty) in model.cal_A:

new_version = 'trip_mip__heur_no_longhaul'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':

else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

# two-thirds from San Diego, regardless of lane cost.
for j in model.j:
    model.x['seattle', j].fix(value(model.b[j]) / 3.0)
    model.x['san-diego', j].fix(value(model.b[j]) * 2.0 / 3.0)

manual.query: Our dispatchers like a dead-simple rule: hand each market entirely to one plant, taking the markets biggest-first and giving each to whichever plant it fits into most snugly on remaining capacity. If we lock the network to that single-source pattern, how much worse is the freight bill than the optimizer's mixed plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('trnsindic_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'best-fit sole-source': handle markets largest-demand-first, assigning each
# entirely to the single plant whose remaining capacity fits it most tightly (smallest
# leftover slack), then let the solver set flow volumes on the fixed arc pattern.
rem = {i: value(model.a[i]) for i in model.i}
for j in sorted(model.j, key=lambda jj: -value(model.b[jj])):
    d = value(model.b[j])
    feasible = [i for i in model.i if rem[i] >= d]
    pick = min(feasible, key=lambda i: rem[i] - d)
    rem[pick] -= d
    for i in model.i:
        model.use[i, j].fix(1 if i == pick else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: best-fit sole-source market-to-plant assignment'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'trnsindic_mip__heur_bestfit', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['trnsindic_mip__heur_bestfit']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Forget the cost-juggling for a second — couldn't we just split each market's order between the two plants in proportion to how big each plant is? Seattle's the smaller cannery, San Diego the bigger, so every city draws from each in that capacity ratio. Wouldn't that simple fair-share rule hold a competitive shipping bill?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('trnsport_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic capacity fair-share policy: allocate each market's demand across the
# plants in proportion to each plant's capacity (a naive split that ignores cost geography).
total_cap = sum(value(model.a[i]) for i in model.i)
for j in model.j:
    for i in model.i:
        model.x[i, j].fix(value(model.b[j]) * value(model.a[i]) / total_cap)
description = 'Heuristic: capacity-proportional fair-share allocation across plants'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'trnsport_lp__heur_fair_share', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['trnsport_lp__heur_fair_share']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Our dispatcher doesn't trust the fancy routing — he just runs the old greedy rule: start at the depot (city 1) and always drive to the nearest stop you haven't hit yet, then loop home at the end. If we lock the tour to that nearest-neighbor route, how much more does it cost than the optimizer's plan?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('tsp2_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic 'nearest neighbor': from the depot, repeatedly hop to the closest
# unvisited city, then close the loop back to the depot; fix x to that route.
cities = list(model.ii)
start = model.depot
unvisited = set(cities)
unvisited.discard(start)
cur = start
route = [start]
while unvisited:
    nxt = min(unvisited, key=lambda j: value(model.c[cur, j]))
    route.append(nxt)
    unvisited.discard(nxt)
    cur = nxt
seq = route + [start]
arcset = set((seq[t], seq[t + 1]) for t in range(len(seq) - 1))
for i in model.ii:
    for j in model.ii:
        if i == j:
            continue
        model.x[i, j].fix(1 if (i, j) in arcset else 0)
description = 'Heuristic: nearest-neighbor tour from the depot'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'tsp2_mip__heur_nn', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['tsp2_mip__heur_nn']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Why fuss over the exact timing of nuclear? Couldn't we just take whatever total nuclear the plan wants and spread it evenly across the periods nuclear is allowed — 1993, 1998 and 2005? What does an even-build schedule like that cost us?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('turkpow_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic nuclear-timing rule: take the total nuclear capacity the optimal plan
# builds and spread it EVENLY across the three periods nuclear is permitted
# (1993, 1998, 2005), instead of letting the model optimize the timing.
tot = sum(value(model.ht['nuclear', t]) for t in model.t)
periods = [1993, 1998, 2005]
share = tot / len(periods)
for t in model.t:
    if t in periods:
        model.ht['nuclear', t].fix(share)
    else:
        model.ht['nuclear', t].fix(0.0)
description = 'Heuristic: even nuclear build across 1993/1998/2005'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, 'turkpow_lp__heur_nuclear_even', models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] {}'.format(models_dictionary['turkpow_lp__heur_nuclear_even']['obj']['value']))

manual.query: Looking across the machines, m3 looks like it's barely pulling its weight while m1 and m2 do most of the work — the standing labor cost on m3 should buy us more of the line. Shouldn't each machine carry at least 30% of total production units, so we're getting the operator headcount we're paying for? What's making the model dump so little on m3?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('uimp_revenue_lp', models_dictionary)
total_prod = sum(model.x[i, j, k, l] for i in model.i for j in model.j for k in model.k for l in model.l)
model.machine_load_floor = ConstraintList()
for l in model.l:
    model.machine_load_floor.add(
        sum(model.x[i, j, k, l] for i in model.i for j in model.j for k in model.k) >= 0.30 * total_prod
    )
description = 'Why-not: each machine must carry >= 30% of total production units for balanced floor utilization'
new_version = 'uimp_revenue_lp__machine_load_floor_30'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)

manual.query: Sales-ops is pushing a make-to-order discipline — set the plan up front to sell exactly the contracted demand in each season, no over-production and no inventory build, and let the solver settle the rest. Wouldn't that be simpler to run and roughly as strong on the topline? What's the model seeing that pulls it the other way?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('uimp_revenue_lp', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: a make-to-order plan set in advance — pin every season's sales to that
# season's contracted demand (no over-production, no inventory build), then solve the residual.
for i in model.i:
    for k in model.k:
        model.z[i, k].fix(value(model.d[i, k]))
new_version = 'uimp_revenue_lp__heur_make_to_order'
description = 'Why-not heuristic: pin sales of every product in every season to contracted demand exactly (make-to-order discipline); solve residual'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    heur_obj = float(models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value'])
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] value={}'.format(heur_obj))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

manual.query: Why not just build out every plant and never worry about capacity — switch on all the ammonia and fertilizer sites and let shipping sort itself out? Wouldn't full coverage be worth it?
manual.constraint:
model = load_model('vietman_vietmip_mip', models_dictionary)
# Heuristic: build out every plant (all fertilizer and ammonia sites open)
for j in model.jd:
    model.z[j].fix(1)
for i in model.id:
    model.w[i].fix(1)
description = 'Why-not heuristic: open every fertilizer and ammonia plant'
new_version = 'vietman_vietmip_mip__heur_open_all'
models_dictionary = solve_model(model, new_version, models_dictionary, tool_context, description)
status = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['sol_status']
if status == 'optimal':
    obj_val = models_dictionary[new_version]['obj']['value']
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={} value={}'.format(status, obj_val))
else:
    print('[HEURISTIC_OBJ] status={}'.format(status))

else:

manual.query: I keep thinking we're overcomplicating this. Quarter 3 has the cheapest selling price ($8) and quarter 2 the dearest ($12) — so why not just run it as a plain buy-low, sell-high play: do all our purchasing in q-3, do all our selling in q-2, and leave q-1 and q-4 out of the trading entirely? Let the solver size the q-3 buy and the q-2 sell however it likes. Wouldn't that simple dedicated schedule be about as good as anything fancier?

Why pour money into kiln expansions at all — couldn't we just run the plant capacity we already have and cover the rest with imports? Wouldn't keeping the existing kilns and skipping all the new builds come out cheaper?
Big kilns feel risky to commit to. Couldn't we just keep things incremental and only ever build small kilns? Wouldn't a small-units-only program be good enough?

deferred.query: Our HR director has been pushing back hard on the temp-heavy summer schedule — she wants total temporary labor across the whole year held to no more than 40 man-days so temps stay a real supplement and not the workhorse. How does that change farm income?
deferred.constraint: model.tlab_annual_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.tlab[tm] for tm in model.tm) <= 40)

deferred.query: Looking at the schedule, temporary labor concentrates in peak months — over 50 man-days in July alone — while family labor stays pinned at full capacity. Couldn't we require that in every month temporary labor never makes up more than 25% of the total labor used that month (family plus temporary)? Shouldn't a smoother labor distribution beat the current plan?

Labor cost looks heavy relative to what the farm earns — eating roughly a third of revenue right now. Wouldn't tying labor spend to a tighter ratio, say no more than 30% of revenue, give us a leaner plan?

The model is cramming nearly all of the good land with crops and leaving the livestock no room there. We'd like to keep some of our best ground in reserve — couldn't we hold cropping on good land to at most 6 hectares so there's slack for grazing or a fallow rotation? What's stopping the model from leaving that buffer?
deferred.constraint: model.good_crop_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.a[p] * model.xcrop[p, 'good'] for (p, s) in model.ps if s == 'good') <= 6.0)

deferred.query: Suppose the link between city 1 and city 5 is unavailable in both directions. Drop that connection and let me see the new assignment cost.
We're looking at a scenario where cities 6 and 7 can't be linked directly to each other either way. Remove that pairing and tell me how the total cost shifts.

Cities 1 and 5 just ping-pong straight off each other in this plan. Couldn't we stop them forming that closed pair and route through them as part of a longer chain? What's pulling them into a two-city loop?

Suppose the manufacturing template wants every die to share the same maximum face number. If we tie the top faces of all three dice to one common value and re-submit, where does the win count come out?
deferred.constraint:
model.equal_tops_12 = Constraint(expr=model.fval['dice1', 'face6'] == model.fval['dice2', 'face6'])
model.equal_tops_23 = Constraint(expr=model.fval['dice2', 'face6'] == model.fval['dice3', 'face6'])

deferred.query: The three dice peak at 16, 13, and 18 — three different ceilings, which seems untidy. Why couldn't they all just top out at the same value without losing wins? Pin every die's highest face to a single shared number and show me the penalty.
deferred.constraint:
model.why_eq_tops_12 = Constraint(expr=model.fval['dice1', 'face6'] == model.fval['dice2', 'face6'])
model.why_eq_tops_23 = Constraint(expr=model.fval['dice2', 'face6'] == model.fval['dice3', 'face6'])

deferred.query: We're weighing whether to put activities act-2 and act-3 in the same building — fac-1 — so they can share a line. What interaction cost does that pairing run us?
Suppose act-3 and act-4 are housed together over in fac-1. Run it and tell me the interaction cost that comes with that.
There's a proposal on the table to share one site between act-1 and act-4, both in fac-1. What's the resulting interaction cost?
Let's try sitting act-1 and act-3 together in fac-1. Where does the interaction cost land?
We're looking at co-locating act-1 and act-5 in the same facility, fac-1. What interaction cost does that bring?

Activities act-2 and act-4 trade a fair bit of work back and forth, yet the layout scatters them to separate buildings. Wouldn't it be obvious to house them together in fac-1? What's the model seeing that keeps them apart?
Act-4 and act-5 feel like they belong side by side given how much they hand off to each other. Shouldn't the plan just drop them both in fac-1? What's the trade-off pulling them to separate sites?
Act-3 and act-5 keep handing material to one another, but the optimal layout splits them up. Couldn't we just keep the two in the same building, fac-1? What's keeping the optimizer from doing that?
Some lanes are carrying very large dose volumes. Shouldn't we cap every plant-to-clinic lane at 300 doses for a more even, lower-risk network? Walk me through what's pulling the plan the other way.
A few delivery lanes carry huge volumes. Couldn't we just cap every press-to-newsstand lane at 250 copies for a more even fleet load? What's the tradeoff I'm missing?
Even 250 feels high. Shouldn't we tighten every lane to 200 copies for a leaner delivery network? Walk me through what's pulling the plan the other way.

Suppose we want a stronger medium-ship presence on the Nakon run for crew-rotation reasons. If we require the medium hulls to lift at least 400 of Nakon's men, what total man-miles does the model come back with?
deferred.constraint: model.nakon_med = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[vv, 'medium', 'nakon'] for (vv, kk, qq) in model.vkp if kk == 'medium' and qq == 'nakon') >= 400)

deferred.query: Roadworks are closing the link between city 4 and city 5 in both directions this season. With that connection gone, what does the best tour come to?
We're evaluating a corridor restriction: no direct legs allowed between the 6/7 depot pair and the 4/5 pair, in either direction. Block that corridor off and tell me how the tour cost shifts.
deferred.constraint: model.block_corridor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[a, b] + model.x[b, a] for a in ['i6', 'i7'] for b in ['i4', 'i5']) == 0)

deferred.query: Suppose the direct connector between city 1 and city 12 is taken out of service both ways. Drop it and tell me what the new best tour costs.

Cities 6 and 7 get visited back to back, like a little twin stop glued together. Wouldn't it be more sensible to split them so each anchors a different stretch of the loop? What's the trade-off that keeps them paired?

The plan is building a big stack of summer inventory just to sell it down in winter, and that ties up bins and working capital we'd rather not carry. Shouldn't we hold end-of-summer carryover to no more than 5 units of each product, instead of building ahead like this? What's the model seeing that makes the build-ahead worth it?
